
# Reading and writing data with Polars

An overview of Polars I/O capabilities:


| Format                                                           	| Implementation         	| Read 	| Write 	| Scan            	|
|------------------------------------------------------------------	|------------------------	|------	|-------	|-----------------	|
| Delimited files (CSV, TSV)                                       	| native                 	| x    	| x     	| x               	|
| Spreadsheets (Excel, ODS)                                        	| via external libraries 	| x    	| x     	|                 	|
| Parquet/Avro/IPC                                                 	| native                 	| x    	| x     	| x               	|
| JSON                                                             	| native                 	| x    	| x     	| x (NDJSON only) 	|
| Database                                                         	| via external libraries 	| x    	| x     	|                 	|
| Cloud storage (AWS S3, Azure blob storage, Google cloud storage) 	| native                 	| x    	| x     	| x               	|


A note regarding `implementation`: A Polars function or method that is entirely written in Rust is referred to as 'native'. Though this definition may not be strictly accurate, it is acceptable
to use it for distinguishing purpose, particularly in contrast to invoking functions/methods from external packages.

A note about `scan`: Similar to the concept of [lazy reading](https://www.tidyverse.org/blog/2021/11/readr-2-1-0-lazy/) in the `readr` package, Polars allows you to `scan` an input file, which defers the actual parsing of the file and provides a `LazyFrame`, a lazy computation holder. This feature offers notable performance benefits:

* reduction of memory usage by reading only the necessary data

* various optimizations by query planner


## Reading data

```{python}
#| include: false
import polars as pl
pl.Config.set_tbl_hide_dataframe_shape(False)
pl.Config.set_tbl_cols(10)
```

### Delimited file (CSV, TSV)

For a complete list of parameter options to use with Polars CSV readers, see this [page](https://docs.pola.rs/api/python/stable/reference/api/polars.read_csv.html). These parameters
provides the same functionality as readr's arguments but with slightly different names. Here's an example:

```{python}
# Read a CSV file
flights_202212 = pl.read_csv(
    source='./data/On_Time_Reporting_Carrier_On_Time_Performance_(1987_present)_2022_12.csv',
    separator=',',                  # single character used to separate fields, default=True
    has_header=True,                # flag indicating whether the first row contains header, default=True
    infer_schema_length=1000,       # maximum number of lines to read for schema inference, default=100
    n_rows=10                       # maximum number of lines to read
)

# Display the first 3 rows
flights_202212.head(3)
```

<br/>

In a complex data set, it is common to override data types for specific columns

```{python}
#| class-source: "numberLines"

# Read the CSV file with specified data types for selected columns
flights_202212 = pl.read_csv(
    source='./data/On_Time_Reporting_Carrier_On_Time_Performance_(1987_present)_2022_12.csv',
    separator=',',                  # single character used to separate fields, default=True
    has_header=True,                # flag the first row has header or not, default=True
    infer_schema_length=1000,       # maximum number of lines to read to infer schema, default=100
    n_rows=10,                      # maximum number of lines to read
    try_parse_dates=True, #<<
    schema_overrides={'Year':pl.Int32, 'Quarter':pl.Int32, 'Month': pl.Int32, 'Reporting_Airline': pl.Categorical} #<<
)

flights_202212.head(3)
```

### Reading multiple files

Polars' `scan_*()` method is a really neat technique for reading multiple files efficiently.

```{python}
from pathlib import Path

def convert_bytes(size):
    """ Convert bytes to KB, or MB or GB"""
    for x in ['bytes', 'KB', 'MB', 'GB', 'TB']:
        if size < 1024.0:
            return "%3.1f %s" % (size, x)
        size /= 1024.0

for p in Path.cwd().rglob('data/On_Time*.csv'):
    print(p.name, ":", convert_bytes(p.stat().st_size))
```

To read multiple files into a single DataFrame, we can use globbing patterns:

```{python}
#| eval: true
#| cache: true
no_flights_each_month = (
    pl.scan_csv('./data/On_Time*.csv')
    .group_by(['Year', 'Month'])
    .agg(
        pl.len().alias('Rows Count')
    )
    .sort('Month')
    .collect()
)

no_flights_each_month
```

If your files don't have to be in a single table you can also build a query plan for each file and execute them in parallel on the Polars thread pool.

All query plan execution is embarrassingly parallel and doesn't require any communication.

```{python}
#| eval: true
#| cache: true
queries = []
for p in Path.cwd().rglob('data/On_Time*.csv'):
    q = (
        pl.scan_csv(p)
        .group_by(['Year', 'Month'])
        .agg(
            pl.len().alias('Rows Count')
        )
    )
    queries.append(q)

dfs = pl.concat(
    pl.collect_all(queries) # this returns a dict of result DataFrame
)
dfs
```

### Reading spreadsheets

Polars reads Excel (`.xlsx`, `.xls`) and ODS files with `read_excel()`. Unlike CSV, Excel requires an external parsing engine — Polars supports three:

| engine | package | notes |
|---|---|---|
| `'calamine'` (default) | `fastexcel` | fastest; Rust-based; recommended for most files |
| `'xlsx2csv'` | `xlsx2csv` | converts to CSV in memory then reads |
| `'openpyxl'` | `openpyxl` | slowest but handles unusual or malformed files |

```r
# R: readxl
library(readxl)
read_excel("flights_summary.xlsx")                      # first sheet
read_excel("flights_summary.xlsx", sheet = "January")  # by name
read_excel("flights_summary.xlsx", sheet = 2)           # by position
```

```{python}
#| eval: false
# Polars: first sheet (default)
pl.read_excel("flights_summary.xlsx")

# specific sheet by name
pl.read_excel("flights_summary.xlsx", sheet_name="January")
```

When a workbook contains multiple sheets, pass `sheet_id=0` to read all sheets at once — Polars returns a dictionary keyed by sheet name:

```{python}
#| eval: false
# read all sheets into a dict of DataFrames
sheets = pl.read_excel("flights_summary.xlsx", sheet_id=0)
january_df = sheets["January"]
```

The equivalent in R is iterating with `purrr::map()` over `readxl::excel_sheets()`:

```r
# R: read all sheets
library(purrr)
path <- "flights_summary.xlsx"
sheets <- excel_sheets(path) |>
    set_names() |>
    map(read_excel, path = path)
```

You can override inferred column types with `schema_overrides=`, matching `readxl`'s `col_types=`:

```{python}
#| eval: false
pl.read_excel(
    "flights_summary.xlsx",
    sheet_name="January",
    schema_overrides={"DepDelay": pl.Float32, "Reporting_Airline": pl.Categorical}
)
```

### Reading a SQL table

Polars can query any SQL database directly, returning a `DataFrame` without going through pandas. There are two functions:

- **`pl.read_database_uri(query, uri)`** — takes a connection string; uses the `connectorx` engine by default (fastest, zero-copy Arrow transfer)
- **`pl.read_database(query, connection)`** — takes an existing DBAPI2 connection object (e.g., `sqlite3`, `psycopg2`, `pyodbc`)

Both are equivalent to R's `DBI::dbGetQuery()` + `dbplyr::collect()`.

```r
# R: DBI + dbplyr
library(DBI)
con <- dbConnect(RPostgres::Postgres(),
                 host = "db.example.com", port = 5432,
                 dbname = "flights", user = "anchu")
tbl(con, "on_time") |>
    filter(OriginStateName == "Ohio") |>
    collect()
dbDisconnect(con)
```

**Using a URI string** (connectorx engine — recommended for PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server):

```{python}
#| eval: false
uri = "postgresql://user:password@host:5432/flights_db"

ohio_flights = pl.read_database_uri(
    query="SELECT * FROM on_time WHERE OriginStateName = 'Ohio'",
    uri=uri
)
```

**Using an existing connection** (works with Python's built-in `sqlite3` and any DBAPI2 driver):

```{python}
import sqlite3

# connect to an in-memory SQLite database as an example
conn = sqlite3.connect(":memory:")
conn.execute("""
    CREATE TABLE on_time (
        FlightDate TEXT, Reporting_Airline TEXT, Origin TEXT, DepDelay REAL
    )
""")
conn.execute("INSERT INTO on_time VALUES ('2022-01-14', 'AA', 'CMH', -3.0)")
conn.execute("INSERT INTO on_time VALUES ('2022-01-15', 'UA', 'ORD', 12.0)")
conn.commit()

result = pl.read_database(
    query="SELECT * FROM on_time WHERE DepDelay > 0",
    connection=conn
)
result
```

```{python}
conn.close()
```

For large result sets, `read_database_uri` supports partitioned parallel reads via `partition_on=`, `partition_range=`, and `partition_num=` — useful when fetching millions of rows from a database with an integer key column.

### Reading S3

Polars reads CSV, Parquet, and other formats directly from S3, GCS, or Azure Blob Storage using the same `read_*` and `scan_*` functions — just swap the local path for a cloud URI:

```{python}
#| eval: false
# read a single Parquet file from S3
df = pl.read_parquet("s3://my-bucket/flights/2022.parquet")

# lazy scan with predicate pushdown (downloads only matching rows)
lazy_df = (
    pl.scan_parquet("s3://my-bucket/flights/*.parquet")
    .filter(pl.col("OriginStateName") == "Ohio")
    .select(["FlightDate", "Reporting_Airline", "DepDelay"])
    .collect()
)
```

Authentication credentials are passed via `storage_options=` or picked up automatically from environment variables (e.g., `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`):

```{python}
#| eval: false
storage_options = {
    "aws_access_key_id":     "YOUR_KEY",
    "aws_secret_access_key": "YOUR_SECRET",
    "aws_region":            "us-east-1",
}

df = pl.scan_parquet(
    "s3://my-bucket/flights/*.parquet",
    storage_options=storage_options
).collect()
```

For GCS and Azure, the URI scheme changes (`gs://` and `az://` respectively) but the API is identical. Install the matching filesystem library (`gcsfs` for GCS, `adlfs` for Azure) alongside `fsspec`.

## Writing data

### Writing CSV

`write_csv()` writes a `DataFrame` to a delimited file. The main parameters map directly to `readr::write_csv()`:

```r
# R: readr
write_csv(full_flights, "output/flights_clean.csv")
write_csv(full_flights, "output/flights_clean.csv.gz")   # auto-compressed
```

```{python}
#| eval: false
# write to CSV
full_flights.write_csv("output/flights_clean.csv")

# write with gzip compression
full_flights.write_csv("output/flights_clean.csv.gz", compression="gzip")
```

Common options:

| parameter | default | purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `separator` | `','` | field delimiter (use `'\t'` for TSV) |
| `include_header` | `True` | write column names as first row |
| `null_value` | `''` | string to write for `null` cells |
| `date_format` | auto | format string for `Date` columns |
| `compression` | `'uncompressed'` | `'gzip'` or `'zstd'` |

### Writing Parquet

Parquet is the recommended format for storing DataFrames on disk: it is columnar, strongly typed, and compresses very efficiently. Writing is a single method call:

```r
# R: arrow
library(arrow)
write_parquet(full_flights, "output/flights.parquet")
```

```{python}
#| eval: false
full_flights.write_parquet("output/flights.parquet")

# choose compression codec (zstd is the default and generally best)
full_flights.write_parquet("output/flights.parquet", compression="snappy")
```

For very large tables, `partition_by=` writes a directory of per-partition Parquet files (Hive-style), which cloud query engines and `scan_parquet()` can then read with partition pruning:

```{python}
#| eval: false
# write one file per carrier: output/Reporting_Airline=AA/data.parquet, etc.
full_flights.write_parquet(
    "output/flights_partitioned/",
    partition_by="Reporting_Airline"
)

# read back with predicate pushdown — only the AA partition is loaded
aa_flights = pl.scan_parquet(
    "output/flights_partitioned/",
    hive_partitioning=True
).filter(pl.col("Reporting_Airline") == "AA").collect()
```

### Writing Excel

`write_excel()` requires `xlsxwriter` (`pip install xlsxwriter`). It produces a formatted `.xlsx` file and supports multiple sheets, column widths, conditional formatting, and sparklines:

```r
# R: writexl
library(writexl)
write_xlsx(full_flights, "output/flights.xlsx")
```

```{python}
#| eval: false
# basic write
full_flights.write_excel("output/flights.xlsx")

# custom sheet name with auto-fitted column widths
full_flights.write_excel(
    "output/flights.xlsx",
    worksheet="January 2022",
    autofit=True
)
```

To write multiple DataFrames to separate sheets in one workbook, pass an `xlsxwriter.Workbook` object:

```{python}
#| eval: false
import xlsxwriter

with xlsxwriter.Workbook("output/flights_multi.xlsx") as wb:
    full_flights.filter(pl.col("OriginStateName") == "Ohio").write_excel(wb, worksheet="Ohio")
    full_flights.filter(pl.col("OriginStateName") == "Texas").write_excel(wb, worksheet="Texas")
```

### Writing to a database

`write_database()` inserts a `DataFrame` into a database table. Pass a connection URI string and choose the engine:

```r
# R: DBI
dbWriteTable(con, "on_time_clean", full_flights)
```

```{python}
#| eval: false
uri = "postgresql://user:password@host:5432/flights_db"

full_flights.write_database(
    table_name="on_time_clean",
    connection=uri,
    if_table_exists="replace"   # 'fail' | 'replace' | 'append'
)
```

The `if_table_exists=` parameter controls behaviour when the target table already exists — equivalent to the `overwrite=` argument in `DBI::dbWriteTable()`.

For SQLite (via the built-in `sqlite3` module and ADBC):

```{python}
#| eval: false
full_flights.write_database(
    table_name="on_time",
    connection="sqlite:///output/flights.db",
    engine="adbc"
)
```

### Writing to S3

`write_parquet()` and `write_csv()` accept S3 (and GCS/Azure) URIs directly, with the same `storage_options=` interface as reading:

```{python}
#| eval: false
full_flights.write_parquet(
    "s3://my-bucket/flights/2022_jan.parquet",
    storage_options={
        "aws_access_key_id":     "YOUR_KEY",
        "aws_secret_access_key": "YOUR_SECRET",
        "aws_region":            "us-east-1",
    }
)
```

For formats not natively supported with cloud URIs (e.g., compressed CSV), use an `fsspec` filesystem object:

```{python}
#| eval: false
import s3fs, gzip

fs = s3fs.S3FileSystem()
with fs.open("s3://my-bucket/flights/2022_jan.csv.gz", "wb") as cloud_f:
    with gzip.open(cloud_f, "w") as f:
        full_flights.write_csv(f)
```
